description: Candidate-budget control over the q-0011 premise-retrieval simulation. e-0012/e-0013 matched bridge vs popularity at equal CLUSTER count k, but cluster count is not a retriever's real cost: clusters differ ~1000x in size (largest hubs ~10-12k members; median cluster 9). For each held-out cross-domain test theorem, walk k=0..15 adding clusters in each method's ranked order and record both cluster-recall and CANDIDATE COST (sum of selected cluster sizes = the pool a retriever must rank). Average over theorems to get a (mean candidate cost -> mean recall) efficiency curve per method; interpolate the candidate budget each needs to reach 0.80/0.90/0.95 recall. W, popularity and sizes from train/full clustering; recall on TEST. Methods: bridge(k), popularity(k), random(k, 20 seeds).
motivation: Matching at cluster-k flatters popularity, whose hubs are the biggest clusters: adding one popularity hub costs ~10k candidate premises vs ~9 for a specialist bridge neighbour. Re-test bridge vs popularity at matched RETRIEVAL COST (candidate count), the metric a real premise retriever actually pays.
formal_results: 432 cross-domain test theorems. At matched candidate budget, bridge reaches each recall target with fewer candidates than popularity: recall>=0.80 bridge 40,834 vs pop 44,523 (-8.3%); >=0.90 59,538 vs 62,319 (-4.5%); >=0.95 72,785 vs 79,807 (-8.8%). Random is cheap (picks tiny clusters: 16,253 candidates at k=15) but ineffective (recall 0.45, never reaches 0.80). Both bridge and popularity need ~60k of 92.6k clustered decls (~64% of Mathlib) in the candidate pool to hit 0.90 cross-domain cluster-recall.
results_description: At matched RETRIEVAL COST (not just cluster count) the bridge ranking is consistently more candidate-efficient than popularity, but only by ~4-9% fewer candidates - both rules converge on the same large hub clusters. The bigger finding: cluster-granular routing is candidate-expensive regardless of routing rule (must pool ~64% of Mathlib for 90% recall), so bridge structure improves WHICH clusters to visit first more than it makes retrieval cheap. Random's low cost is an artifact of picking tiny clusters that rarely hold the needed premises.
conclusions: Refines a-0013: the e-0012 cluster-k near-tie does NOT reverse into a large bridge win once retrieval cost is measured properly - bridge beats popularity by a modest, consistent 4-9% candidate-budget margin at every recall target (directionally confirming real bridge signal, consistent with the e-0013 specialist effect). But cluster-level routing is intrinsically coarse and candidate-heavy (~64% of the library needed for 90% recall), so the operational payoff of bridge structure is 'visit the right clusters slightly sooner', not 'retrieve cheaply'; intra-cluster premise ranking is the missing piece. Honest, leakage-controlled cost accounting that bounds the q-0011 payoff claim.
